text: This video demonstrates single sitting robotic retroperitoneal pelvic and inguinal lymphadenectomy. A fifty seven year old gentleman presented with enlarged hard and fixed right inguinal lymph nodes. The true cut biopsy revealed poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. He had a prior history of excision of eccrine carcinoma of the right flank in two thousand nine. He was disease free on yearly follow ups. A PET-CT scan was done, which revealed metastasis in parietic atriocaval, retrocaval, paracavel, right common iliac, right inguinal, and left cervical lymph nodes. The cytology from left cervical node was suggestive of squamous carcinomatous deposits.

In view of the good performance status of the patient and positive treatment guidelines for this extremely rare neoplasm, it was decided to proceed with simultaneous robotic retroperitoneal right pelvic and right inguinal lymphadenectomy. An informed consent was obtained from the patient regarding the non-curative nature of the surgery. The da Vinci X-ray system was used for the procedure. The patient was placed in the Tenderlenburg position for the retropaternal and the pelvic part of the surgery. A split leg position was employed for the inguinal lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon places ports in a horizontal line for bilateral retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND), with a 12mm assistant port on the right and another in the suprapubic area. The peritoneum medial to the cecum is incised, and peritoneal folds are lifted using nylon sutures. The right common iliac lymph nodes are dissected up to the vena cava, where a rent is repaired with 3-0 proline sutures. Dissection proceeds cranially, separating lymph nodes from major vessels using the split and roll method. Lumbar vessels are divided between hemlock clips, and paracaval lymph nodes are removed en bloc. Intra-aortocaval and pre-aortic lymph nodes are dissected, but the left renal artery is injured at the ostium level.

The surgeon uses the left robotic instrument to apply compression while repairing a rent with a three-oh proline suture. An intraoperative Doppler confirms pulsatile flow in the left renal artery post-repair. Small vessels and lymphatic channels are divided between clips. The para-aortic lymph nodes are further dissected. During dissection, the inferior mesenteric artery is inadvertently injured by the robotic instrument, clipped, and its ostium is suture repaired using a three-oh proline rescue stitch.

The surgeon loops the aorta and dissects the inter-aortocaval lymph nodes, removing them before proceeding cranially to identify the right renal artery. The retrocaval and paracaval lymph nodes are also removed. The final view shows the great vessels after retroperitoneal dissection. The camera is rotated towards the right pelvis, where extended pelvic lymph node dissection is performed, removing enlarged lymph nodes around the iliac artery and vein. The dissection continues distally over the iliac vessels, concluding with a final view of the right pelvis post-lymphadenectomy.

The robotic system is repositioned to subcutaneous ports for the right inguinal lymphadenectomy. Dissection begins superficially to the fascia lata, extending to the inguinal ligament. The fascia lata is opened to access the femoral vessels, where the tributaries and main trunk of the great saphenous vein are divided. An accidental rent in the right femoral artery is repaired with a three-oh proline stitch. Dissection proceeds cranially, carefully avoiding thinning and button-holing of the skin. The clock-face lymph node is removed, and the nodal packet is placed in a specimen bag. The femoral ring is closed.

The total consult time was four sixty-five minutes and the estimated blood loss was three hundred milliliters. The patient did not require any intraoperative or postoperative blood transfusion. He was discharged on the third postoperative day with a groin drain which was removed after twelve days. Seventy-eight out of the seventy-nine lymph nodes removed were positive for poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The patient was started on cycloposphamide, adriamycin, cisplatin chemotherapy and is doing well at two months of follow-up.

This video is presented to emphasize the minimally invasive management of vascular complications of retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy and inguinal lymphadenectomy. These complications can be very well managed using the robot without compromising the oncological outcomes. The importance of a solid and skilled robotic team cannot be underestimated.
